title: Snowflake Integration
code: sql <- "   SELECT     REGR_SLOPE(sales, price) AS slope,     REGR_COUNT(sales, price) AS n_obs,     -- Compute SE: sqrt(MSE / SXX)     SQRT(       REGR_SYYX(sales, price) / NULLIF(REGR_COUNT(sales, price) - 2, 0)       / NULLIF(REGR_SXX(sales, price), 0)     ) AS slope_se   FROM sales_data   WHERE category = 'Electronics'     AND sale_date >= '2024-01-01' "  result <- curve_snowflake(con, sql, type = "regression") ggcurve(result[[1]], nullvalue = 0, title = "Price Effect on Electronics Sales")

title: Snowflake Integration
text: standard error slope : \[SE(\hat{\beta}_1) = \sqrt{\frac{MSE}{S_{XX}}} = \sqrt{\frac{SSR/(n-2)}{S_{XX}}}\] ## /B Test Results conversion rate experiments:
code: sql <- "   SELECT     COUNT_IF(variant = 'control') AS n_control,     COUNT_IF(variant = 'control' AND converted) AS x_control,     COUNT_IF(variant = 'treatment') AS n_treatment,     COUNT_IF(variant = 'treatment' AND converted) AS x_treatment   FROM experiment_events   WHERE experiment_id = 'checkout_v2'     AND event_date BETWEEN '2024-06-01' AND '2024-06-30' "  result <- curve_snowflake(con, sql, type = "abtest")  # The curve shows the difference in conversion rates (treatment - control) ggcurve(   result[[1]],   nullvalue = 0,   title = "Checkout Redesign: Conversion Rate Lift",   xaxis = "Difference in Conversion Rate" )  # Check S-value at null svalue_at_null(result[[1]], null = 0)

code: sql <- "   SELECT     odds_ratio AS estimate,     or_lower_95 AS lower_ci,     or_upper_95 AS upper_ci   FROM survival_analysis_results   WHERE cohort = '2024_q1'     AND outcome = 'churn_90d' "  result <- curve_snowflake(   con, sql,   type = "ratio",   ci_level = 0.95 )  ggcurve(   result[[1]],   measure = "ratio",   nullvalue = 1,   title = "Churn Risk: Treatment vs Control" )

code: sql <- "   SELECT     DATE_TRUNC('month', analysis_date)::VARCHAR AS group_name,     elasticity_estimate AS estimate,     elasticity_se AS std_error,     n_transactions - 2 AS df   FROM analytics.monthly_elasticity   WHERE product_category = 'Apparel'     AND analysis_date >= '2023-01-01'   ORDER BY analysis_date "  monthly_curves <- curve_snowflake_batch(con, sql, type = "se")  # Select specific months for comparison selected <- monthly_curves[c("2024-01", "2024-04", "2024-07", "2024-10")] plot_multi(selected, nullvalue = -1, title = "Elasticity Trend (Quarterly)")

what: Pattern 1: Lift Measurement with Bootstrap SE
title: Snowflake Integration
code: -- Compute lift with standard error from bootstrapped samples WITH daily_metrics AS (   SELECT   sale_date,   SUM(CASE WHEN in_treatment THEN revenue END) AS treatment_rev,   SUM(CASE WHEN NOT in_treatment THEN revenue END) AS control_rev,   COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN in_treatment THEN customer_id END) AS treatment_n,   COUNT(DISTINCT CASE WHEN NOT in_treatment THEN customer_id END) AS control_n   FROM sales   WHERE campaign_id = 'test_123'   GROUP BY sale_date ) SELECT AVG(treatment_rev / NULLIF(treatment_n, 0) - control_rev / NULLIF(control_n, 0)) AS estimate, STDDEV(treatment_rev / NULLIF(treatment_n, 0) - control_rev / NULLIF(control_n, 0)) / SQRT(COUNT(*)) AS std_error, COUNT(*) - 1 AS df FROM daily_metrics

title: Snowflake Integration
code: -- Weighted difference between groups with pooled variance WITH group_stats AS (   SELECT   treatment_group,   AVG(outcome) AS mean_outcome,   VARIANCE(outcome) AS var_outcome,   COUNT(*) AS n   FROM experiment_data   GROUP BY treatment_group ) SELECT MAX(CASE WHEN treatment_group = 1 THEN mean_outcome END) -   MAX(CASE WHEN treatment_group = 0 THEN mean_outcome END) AS estimate,  SQRT(   SUM(var_outcome * (n - 1)) / (SUM(n) - 2) *  -- Pooled variance   (1.0 / MAX(CASE WHEN treatment_group = 1 THEN n END) +       1.0 / MAX(CASE WHEN treatment_group = 0 THEN n END)) ) AS std_error,  SUM(n) - 2 AS df FROM group_stats

title: Snowflake Integration
code: -- Relative lift with delta method SE WITH stats AS (   SELECT   AVG(CASE WHEN variant = 'treatment' THEN metric END) AS mu_t,   AVG(CASE WHEN variant = 'control' THEN metric END) AS mu_c,   VARIANCE(CASE WHEN variant = 'treatment' THEN metric END) AS var_t,   VARIANCE(CASE WHEN variant = 'control' THEN metric END) AS var_c,   COUNT_IF(variant = 'treatment') AS n_t,   COUNT_IF(variant = 'control') AS n_c   FROM experiment_results ) SELECT mu_t / NULLIF(mu_c, 0) AS estimate, -- 95% CI via delta method (mu_t / NULLIF(mu_c, 0)) * (1 - 1.96 * SQRT(var_t / (n_t * mu_t * mu_t) +                                               var_c / (n_c * mu_c * mu_c))) AS lower_ci, (mu_t / NULLIF(mu_c, 0)) * (1 + 1.96 * SQRT(var_t / (n_t * mu_t * mu_t) +                                               var_c / (n_c * mu_c * mu_c))) AS upper_ci FROM stats

title: Troubleshooting
text: package computing thousands interval estimates via iterations bootstrapping, requires lot computational power. Luckily, concurve supports parallelization, although disabled default users, use Windows, unable use . However, able use parallelization, can enable following script script detect number cores machine via parallel package use speed computations, especially bootstrapping. However, like speed computations unable use parallelization, can reduce number steps concurve functions, drastically reduce time takes complete operation. default, steps arguments set 10000. example, changed number steps 100, minimum needed plot function, process now much quicker. can evaluate using microbenchmark. use bench package mark() function. using parallelization, must also set memory argument FALSE. ’ll now enable parallelization setting mc.cores option detect max number cores available using detectCores(). mc.cores option argument almost curve functions concurve package use parallel computing. setting number iterations 100, utilizing parallelization doesn’t seem help much, setting number iterations 500000 using multiple cores, seems computational advantage.
code: library(parallel) options(mc.cores = 1) library(bench) library(parallel) options(mc.cores = 1) getOption("mc.cores", 1L) set.seed(1031) func1 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 100 ), memory = FALSE)  func2 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 500000 ), memory = FALSE) #> Warning: Some expressions had a GC in every iteration; so filtering is #> disabled. getOption("mc.cores", 1L) set.seed(1031) func3 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 100 ), memory = FALSE)  func4 <- mark(df1 <- curve_rev(   point = 1.61, LL = 0.997, UL = 2.59,   measure = "ratio", steps = 500000 ), memory = FALSE) #> Warning: Some expressions had a GC in every iteration; so filtering is #> disabled. func1$median #> [1] 713µs func2$median #> [1] 1.11s func3$median #> [1] 784µs func4$median #> [1] 968ms

Here "FE" is the fixed-effects model  res <- rma(yi, vi,   data = dat, slab = paste(StudyName, sep = ", "),   method = "FE", digits = 2 )  # Calculate the intervals using the metainterval function  metaf <- curve_meta(res) } # }

code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ library(concurve)  GroupA <- rnorm(500) GroupB <- rnorm(500)  RandomData <- data.frame(GroupA, GroupB)  intervalsdf <- curve_mean(GroupA, GroupB, data = RandomData, method = "default")  (z <- curve_table(intervalsdf[[1]], format = "data.frame")) (z <- curve_table(intervalsdf[[1]], format = "latex")) (z <- curve_table(intervalsdf[[1]], format = "image")) } # }

text: data dataframe produced one interval functions intervals stored. type Choose whether plot "consonance" function, "surprisal" function "likelihood". default option set "c". type must set quotes, example ggcurve (type = "s") ggcurve(type = "c"). options include "pd" consonance distribution function, "cd" consonance density function, "l1" relative likelihood, "l2" log-likelihood, "l3" likelihood "d" deviance function. measure Indicates whether object log transformation normal/default. default setting "default". measure set "ratio", take logarithmically transformed values convert back normal values dataframe. typically setting used binary outcomes measures risk ratios, hazard ratios, odds ratios. levels Indicates interval levels plotted function. default set 0.95 plot 95% interval consonance function, levels can plotted using c() function example, levels = c(0.50, 0.75, 0.95). nullvalue Indicates whether null value measure plotted. default, set NULL, meaning plotted vertical line. Changing numerical vector specify region line plotted area shaded. input must numerical vector, example c(-0.5, 0.5) single numerical vector 0 1. position Determines orientation P-value (consonance) function. default, set "pyramid", meaning p-value function stand right side , like pyramid. However, can also inverted via option "inverted". also change sequence y-axes match orientation.can set , ggcurve(type = "c", data = df, position = "inverted"). title custom title graph. default, set "Consonance Function". order set title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, title = "Custom Title"). subtitle custom subtitle graph. default, set "function contains consonance/confidence intervals every level P-values." order set subtitle, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, subtitle = "Custom Subtitle"). xaxis custom x-axis title graph. default, set "Range Values. order set x-axis title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, xaxis = "Hazard Ratio"). yaxis1 custom y-axis title graph. default, set "Consonance Level". order set y-axis title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, yxis1= "Confidence Level"). yaxis2 custom y-axis title graph. default, set "Levels CI". order set y-axis title, must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, yxis2= "Confidence Level"). color Item allows user choose color points ribbons graph. default, set color = "#555555". inputs must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, color = "#333333"). fill Item allows user choose color ribbons graph. default, set fill = "#239a98". inputs must quotes. example, ggcurve(type = "c", data = x, fill = "#333333").
